content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS of length NNN. He wonders if it is possible to divide SSS into exactly KKK non-empty substrings such that each SiS_iSi​ belongs to exactly one substring and the XOR\texttt{XOR}XOR of each substring is the same. Can you help Chef to determine if it is possible to do so?

Note: XOR\texttt{XOR}XOR of substring SL⋯RS_{L \cdots R}SL⋯R​ is defined as: XOR(SL⋯R)=SL⊕SL+1⊕⋯⊕SR\texttt{XOR} (S_{L \cdots R}) = S_L \oplus S_{L+1} \oplus \cdots \oplus S_RXOR(SL⋯R​)=SL​⊕SL+1​⊕⋯⊕SR​.

Here, ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and KKK - the length of the binary string SSS and the number of substrings in which SSS has to be divided.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if SSS can be divided into KKK substrings such that XOR\texttt{XOR}XOR of each substring is the same. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line has two integers N and K, the length of the binary string and the required number of substrings. The second line contains a binary string S of length N consisting of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to split S into exactly K non‑empty substrings whose XOR (parity of number of 1's) is identical, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: It can be proven that there is no way to divide S=111S = 111S=111 into K=2K = 2K=2 substrings such that XOR\texttt{XOR}XOR of each substring is the same.
Test case 2: One of the possible ways of dividing SSS is: 0‾\underline{0}0​ 11‾\underline{11}11​ 00‾\underline{00}00​. Here XOR\texttt{XOR}XOR of each substring is 000.
Test case 3: One of the possible ways of dividing SSS is: 01‾\underline{01}01​ 001‾\underline{001}001​ 111‾\underline{111}111​. Here XOR\texttt{XOR}XOR of each substring is 111.
Test case 4: It can be proven that there is no way to divide S=000100S = 000100S=000100 into K=2K = 2K=2 substrings such that XOR\texttt{XOR}XOR of each substring is the same.

solution.approach:
For a binary substring the XOR equals the parity of the number of '1's. Let totalParity be the parity of the whole string. If K is odd, the common parity p must equal totalParity; if K is even, totalParity must be 0 and p can be either 0 or 1. We need K‑1 cuts before the end, each producing a segment with parity p. While scanning the string we compute the prefix parity for every position i (1‑based). A cut can be placed after i if prefixParity(i) == p. Therefore we count how many positions i (1 ≤ i < N) have prefixParity 0 (cnt0) and how many have 1 (cnt1). The answer is:
- If K is odd: check if cnt_{totalParity} ≥ K‑1.
- If K is even: totalParity must be 0 and check if max(cnt0, cnt1) ≥ K‑1.
If the condition holds, output YES, otherwise NO. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(ΣN) ≤ 2·10^5
